1: These are the stages of the Israelites when they came out of the land of Egypt by their companies under the leadership of Moses and Aaron. The comprehensive wilderness itinerary is a memorial of God's faithfulness through every stage of the 40-year journey. Christ himself is described as accompanying Israel throughout: 'They drank from the spiritual rock that followed them, and that rock was Christ' (1 Cor 10:4). The stages are not random wandering but purposeful, Spirit-guided movement.
2: Moses recorded their starting places according to their journeys at the command of the LORD. The record is written by divine command — a permanent memorial of the covenant journey. The Lamb's book of life is the ultimate divine record: every name, every stage, every moment of the redeemed community written and kept by God (Rev 20:12; Phil 4:3).
3: They set out from Rameses in the first month, on the fifteenth day of the first month — the day after the Passover. The entire wilderness journey begins with Passover. The Christian life similarly begins with the true Passover: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Let us therefore celebrate the festival' (1 Cor 5:7–8). Every stage that follows is post-Passover — lived in the light of Christ's atoning death.
4: The Israelites went out boldly while the Egyptians were burying their firstborn, whom the LORD had struck down. YHWH executed judgments against their gods. The exodus departs on the very day Egypt mourns its dead firstborn, while YHWH has triumphed over Egypt's divine system. Christ's resurrection is the ultimate triumph over all rival lordships: 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them in it' (Col 2:15).
5: The Israelites set out from Rameses and camped at Succoth. Succoth means 'booths' — the first encampment after Egypt recalls the Feast of Booths, the festival of wilderness dwelling. Christ 'tabernacled' (eskēnōsen) among us (John 1:14), the one whose presence with his people is the substance of every 'booth' festival.
6: They set out from Succoth and camped at Etham on the edge of the wilderness. The edge of the wilderness — the threshold between the cultivated world and the unknown — is the liminal space of faith. Christ leads his people into the wilderness of this age, away from the securities of the old world and toward the inheritance of the new (Heb 11:38).
7: They set out from Etham and turned back to Pi-hahiroth, which is east of Baal-zephon, and they camped before Migdol. The apparent reversal of direction at Etham — turning back toward Egypt — looks like retreat. God's leading sometimes appears counterintuitive, placing his people in positions where only divine deliverance can save them. Christ's path to glory went through apparent defeat; the disciples' path likewise often involves turning back before advancing.
8: They set out from before Pi-hahiroth and passed through the sea into the wilderness. They traveled three days in the wilderness of Etham and camped at Marah. The crossing of the sea is the central redemptive act of the exodus, and the three-day journey to Marah mirrors Christ's three-day journey through death. The sea crossing is explicitly applied to baptism by Paul: 'baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea' (1 Cor 10:1–2).
9: They set out from Marah and came to Elim — twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees — and camped there. After bitter water comes the oasis of abundance: twelve springs (one for each tribe) and seventy palms (the number of the nations). The pattern of bitter then sweet, testing then blessing, runs through the whole wilderness journey and the whole Christian life. After every Marah, Christ provides an Elim (Rom 8:18).
10: They set out from Elim and camped by the Red Sea. The encampment by the sea — already crossed — is a moment of return to the site of redemption. The Lord's Supper similarly returns the community repeatedly to the site of their redemption, proclaiming Christ's death until he comes (1 Cor 11:26).
11: They set out from the Red Sea and camped in the wilderness of Sin. Sin is the wilderness where manna was first given — the daily bread from heaven. Christ is the true manna, the bread of life who came down from heaven so that whoever eats of him may live forever (John 6:31–35). Every 'wilderness of Sin' — every place of scarcity — is where Christ feeds his people.
12: They set out from the wilderness of Sin and camped at Dophkah. Each named encampment in the list is a stage in a purposeful journey — God directing every movement, allowing no accidental detour. Christ's people are similarly guided: 'In your book were written, every one of them, the days that were formed for me, when as yet there was none of them' (Ps 139:16).
13: They set out from Dophkah and camped at Alush. The names of the encampments, though many are obscure, are all recorded. God's word records the obscure stages alongside the famous ones — Sinai and the Red Sea alongside unnamed Dophkah and Alush. Christ is present in the obscure as well as the famous stages of his people's journey, seeing every stage as significant (Matt 10:29–30).
14: They set out from Alush and camped at Rephidim — where there was no water for the people to drink. Rephidim is where Moses struck the rock and water flowed. Paul makes the connection explicit: 'they drank from the spiritual rock that followed them, and that rock was Christ' (1 Cor 10:4). Every Rephidim of thirst is an occasion for Christ to be the living water.
15: They set out from Rephidim and camped in the wilderness of Sinai. Sinai — the mountain of divine revelation and covenant — is the climactic encampment of the first part of the journey. Christ is the mediator of a better covenant, established on better promises (Heb 8:6), the one who fulfills what Sinai's covenant pointed toward.
16: They set out from the wilderness of Sinai and camped at Kibroth-hattaavah ('graves of craving'). Kibroth-hattaavah is where the people craved meat and were struck with plague while still eating the quail. The danger of spiritual craving — desiring to return to Egypt's fleshpots rather than trusting God's provision — is a warning Christ applies: 'One does not live by bread alone' (Matt 4:4).
17: They set out from Kibroth-hattaavah and camped at Hazeroth. Hazeroth is where Miriam and Aaron opposed Moses, and Miriam was struck with leprosy. The encampment that should have been peaceful became the site of leadership conflict. Christ himself faced opposition from those closest to him; the lesson of Hazeroth is that opposition to God's appointed mediator brings judgment, while intercession (as Moses interceded for Miriam) brings healing (Num 12:1–15; Heb 7:25).
18: They set out from Hazeroth and camped at Rithmah. Rithmah (meaning 'broom plant') — a place of desert vegetation, the same plant under which Elijah rested in despair (1 Kgs 19:4–5) before God fed him. God's provision comes in the most desert places; Christ meets the weary at their broom-plant moment of despair and sustains them for the journey ahead.
19: They set out from Rithmah and camped at Rimmon-perez. Rimmon-perez means 'pomegranate of the breach/pass.' The pomegranate was one of the good fruits of the Promised Land — brought back by the spies alongside the cluster of grapes. Every encampment name carries seeds of the promised inheritance even in the wilderness; Christ is the first fruits of the new creation (1 Cor 15:20), the pomegranate of the pass tasted even now.
20: They set out from Rimmon-perez and camped at Libnah. Libnah means 'whiteness/purity.' The pursuit of purity — holiness in the wilderness — is a recurring theme of Israel's journey. Christ is the one who makes his people white: 'Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow' (Isa 1:18; Rev 7:14, robes washed white in the Lamb's blood).
21: They set out from Libnah and camped at Rissah. The journey continues through obscure encampments. The faithful repetition of 'they set out... and camped' models the rhythm of Christian obedience: move when called, rest when permitted, neither rushing ahead nor lagging behind the divine pillar of cloud (Num 9:17–23).
22: They set out from Rissah and camped at Kehelathah. Kehelathah means 'assembly/congregation.' The community assembled at every encampment — not just marching as individuals but gathering as a people. The church's assembly is the living Kehelathah: every gathering 'in my name' is the new covenant encampment where Christ is present (Matt 18:20).
23: They set out from Kehelathah and camped at Mount Shepher. Shepher means 'beauty/pleasantness.' Even in the wilderness journey, there are places of beauty — mountains that lift the eyes to God's creation. Christ restores beauty to his people's journey: 'How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news' (Rom 10:15; Isa 52:7).
24: They set out from Mount Shepher and camped at Haradah. Haradah means 'trembling/terror.' The encampment of trembling marks the moments when the wilderness's dangers were most acute. Christ meets his people at every Haradah moment: 'Fear not, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by name, you are mine' (Isa 43:1).
25: They set out from Haradah and camped at Makheloth. Makheloth means 'assemblies/congregations.' The double assembly name suggests a gathering-of-gatherings — the community of communities. Christ's body is a gathering of all the gatherings: 'one body and one Spirit, just as you were called to the one hope that belongs to your call' (Eph 4:4).
26: They set out from Makheloth and camped at Tahath. Tahath means 'beneath/below/lowliness.' The encampment of lowliness is the place of humility. Christ's entire incarnate life was a descent into lowliness: 'He humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross' (Phil 2:8). The encampment at Tahath marks the pattern of descent that precedes every exaltation.
27: They set out from Tahath and camped at Terah. Terah — the name of Abraham's father — suggests a return to origins, a connection to the patriarchal roots of the journey. Christ fulfills the Abrahamic covenant that Terah's line carried: 'In your offspring shall all the nations of the earth be blessed' (Gen 22:18; Gal 3:16).
28: They set out from Terah and camped at Mithkah. Mithkah means 'sweetness.' After the trembling and lowliness, a place of sweetness — the honey in the rock (Ps 81:16), the manna's taste of honey and oil (Num 11:8). Christ's words are sweeter than honey (Ps 119:103); he promises to feed his people from the hidden manna (Rev 2:17).
29: They set out from Mithkah and camped at Hashmonah. Hashmonah — possibly connected to Hashmonean names — is a site of transition. The wilderness journey passes through many transitional moments where one era ends and another begins. Christ is the one who stands at every such threshold: 'I am the door' (John 10:9), the passage-point for every divine transition.
30: They set out from Hashmonah and camped at Moseroth. Moseroth was near Mount Hor — a place associated with Aaron's death in other traditions. The shadow of mortality falls across the wilderness journey; the wilderness generation is always living toward its appointed end. Christ transforms the encounter with mortality: 'I am the resurrection and the life; whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live' (John 11:25).
31: They set out from Moseroth and camped at Bene-jaakan. Bene-jaakan means 'sons of Jaakan' — a clan of the Horites/Edomites, a people encountered on the wilderness edges. The covenant community passes through the territories of neighboring peoples, learning to distinguish allies from adversaries, to live among the nations without being consumed by them (John 17:15).
32: They set out from Bene-jaakan and camped at Hor-haggidgad. Hor-haggidgad (variant spelling of Gudgodah) — a deep valley encampment. The valleys of the wilderness journey are as purposeful as the mountaintops. Christ walks through the valley of the shadow of death with his people, his rod and staff bringing comfort (Ps 23:4).
33: They set out from Hor-haggidgad and camped at Jotbathah — a land with flowing streams. After the valley, flowing water. The rhythm of dry and flowing, desert and stream, runs through the whole wilderness narrative. Christ is the living water who turns desert places into rivers: 'Whoever believes in me, as Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from within him' (John 7:38).
34: They set out from Jotbathah and camped at Abronah. The journey continues. The steady progression — regardless of named obstacles or blessings at each site — models the perseverance that the letter to the Hebrews commends: 'Run with endurance the race that is set before us' (Heb 12:1), not stopping at either trial or triumph.
35: They set out from Abronah and camped at Ezion-geber. Ezion-geber on the Red Sea coast — a port city, point of maritime connection to the wider world. Christ's gospel is for all nations, carried outward from the covenant community to every coast: 'to the ends of the earth' (Acts 1:8). The wilderness journey reaches the sea — the boundary of the known world — and points beyond.
36: They set out from Ezion-geber and camped in the wilderness of Zin (that is, Kadesh). Kadesh — the place of the great crisis, the refusal to enter the land. The itinerary passes through Kadesh again, naming the site of the generation's decisive failure. The church is called to remember its failures not in shame but in warning: 'see to it that no one fails to obtain the grace of God' (Heb 12:15).
37: They set out from Kadesh and camped at Mount Hor on the edge of the land of Edom. Mount Hor — where Aaron dies — is the penultimate encampment before the final approach to Canaan. The death of the high priest at the threshold of the Promised Land points forward to Christ's death at the threshold of the new covenant: the high priest must die before the inheritance is distributed (Heb 9:16–17, 'a will takes effect only at death').
38: Aaron the priest went up Mount Hor at the command of the LORD and died there, in the fortieth year after the Israelites had come out of Egypt, on the first day of the fifth month. Aaron's death by divine command, on an appointed date, at an appointed place — with his son Eleazar succeeding him — demonstrates that all things in God's redemptive plan occur according to a divine timetable. Christ died 'at the right time' (Rom 5:6, katà kairon), according to the Father's appointed schedule.
39: Aaron was 123 years old when he died on Mount Hor. Aaron's full lifespan of 123 years — a life of service, sin, forgiveness, and faithful priestly work — was lived entirely in preparation for and execution of the Levitical priesthood. Christ's priestly life, though only 33 years, accomplished what Aaron's 123 could only symbolize (Heb 9:11–12).
40: The Canaanite king of Arad, who lived in the Negeb in the land of Canaan, heard that the Israelites were coming — a note inserted here (see Numbers 21:1-3). The advance warning to the enemy is a feature of God's providential preparation: news of Israel's approach precedes them, just as the fame of Christ goes before him: 'the fragrance of the knowledge of him everywhere' (2 Cor 2:14).
41: They set out from Mount Hor and camped at Zalmonah. The post-Aaron journey continues under Eleazar's priesthood — the transition from one era to the next accomplished smoothly at divine command. The church always continues past the death of its leaders because Christ himself never dies; the ministry does not depend on any individual priest but on the eternal High Priest (Heb 7:24).
42: They set out from Zalmonah and camped at Punon. Punon — a copper-mining district — is the site associated with the bronze-serpent incident (Numbers 21). The encampment at Punon recalls the judgment and healing at the same site: fiery serpents, death, intercession, and the life-giving bronze serpent that Jesus applies to himself (John 3:14–15).
43: They set out from Punon and camped at Oboth. Oboth means 'water-skins' — vessels for carrying water through the desert. The community carries what they need for the journey ahead; Christ calls his people to carry living water to the dry places of the world: 'whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst' (John 4:14).
44: They set out from Oboth and camped at Iye-abarim, on the border of Moab. The Abarim range is where Moses will later see the Promised Land and die. The itinerary moves inexorably toward both the promised inheritance and the appointed death of the mediator — two things that belong together. Christ's death precedes his people's full entry into the inheritance he has secured.
45: They set out from Iyim and camped at Dibon-gad. Dibon becomes associated with the tribe of Gad — a reminder that every encampment site is eventually inherited and named by a covenant family. The entire wilderness journey transforms from a record of wandering into a deed of ownership: every place where God's people camped becomes territory claimed for the covenant community.
46: They set out from Dibon-gad and camped at Almon-diblathaim. The journey is nearing its end — the final encampments before the plains of Moab and the Jordan crossing. As every race has a final stretch, Christ promises endurance to those who run to the finish: 'The one who endures to the end will be saved' (Matt 24:13).
47: They set out from Almon-diblathaim and camped in the mountains of Abarim, before Nebo. Mount Nebo — where Moses will die and see the Promised Land — is the penultimate encampment. The vision before the end: the land seen but not yet entered. Christ's people 'see him who is invisible' by faith (Heb 11:27), glimpsing the inheritance ahead of full possession.
48: They set out from the mountains of Abarim and camped in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho. The final encampment of the itinerary — across from Jericho, on the threshold of crossing. The journey of 40 years ends where it was always meant to end: at the Jordan, ready to enter. Christ's pilgrimage people are always moving toward this threshold: 'Here we have no lasting city, but we seek the city that is to come' (Heb 13:14).
49: They camped by the Jordan from Beth-jeshimoth as far as Abel-shittim in the plains of Moab. The final encampment stretches along the Jordan — the whole community poised for crossing. The Jordan is the traditional symbol of death and passage into the inheritance. Christ crosses Jordan first and stands on the other side, the risen pioneer who calls his people across: 'I am the resurrection and the life' (John 11:25).
50: In the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, the LORD speaks to Moses. The divine word comes at the threshold — instruction for the crossing and the conquest. Christ speaks to his people at every threshold moment of the kingdom's advance, commissioning them for what comes next: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go therefore...' (Matt 28:18–19).
51: Speak to the Israelites and say to them: When you cross the Jordan into the land of Canaan. The conditional 'when you cross' takes the crossing for granted — it is an assumption, not a hope. Christ's promises to his people are similarly certain: 'I will come again and will take you to myself, that where I am you may be also' (John 14:3) — not 'if I come' but 'I will come.'
52: You shall drive out all the inhabitants of the land from before you and destroy all their figured stones and destroy all their metal images and demolish all their high places. The total spiritual cleansing of the land — every idol, every high place, every cult image — models Christ's call for total sanctification of the life. Paul applies this directly: 'What fellowship has light with darkness? Therefore go out from their midst, and be separate' (2 Cor 6:14–17).
53: And you shall take possession of the land and settle in it, for I have given the land to you to possess it. The divine gift precedes and guarantees the human task: 'I have given the land to you' — past tense — before they have set foot in it. Christ secures the inheritance before his people can claim it: 'I go to prepare a place for you' (John 14:2), and the preparation is complete before the arrival.
54: You shall inherit the land by lot according to your clans. To a large group give a large inheritance, and to a small group give a small inheritance. Wherever the lot falls for anyone, that shall be his; you shall inherit according to your ancestral tribes. Both divine sovereignty (lot) and human proportionality govern the distribution. Christ distributes gifts and callings 'as he wills' (1 Cor 12:11), not according to human status but according to divine wisdom.
55: But if you do not drive out the inhabitants of the land from before you, then those you allow to remain shall be thorns in your eyes and barbs in your sides, and they shall trouble you in the land where you dwell. The warning against spiritual accommodation: partial obedience produces ongoing torment. Christ calls for radical surgery on sin: 'If your eye causes you to sin, tear it out. It is better for you to enter the kingdom of God with one eye than with two eyes to be thrown into hell' (Mark 9:47).
56: And I will do to you what I thought to do to them. The reversal warning: Israel risks becoming the object of the same judgment it was sent to execute. The church faces the same warning — called to holy living, it can itself become the thing it was meant to judge if it accommodates the world's idolatries. Christ calls his people to ongoing vigilance: 'Let anyone who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall' (1 Cor 10:12).
